items.foco: Definition: obstruction of the drainage of the aqueous humor
items.title: Pathophysiological definition
items.contentHtml:
Definition: obstruction of aqueous humor drainage

  • Angle-closure glaucoma is defined as the obstruction of aqueous humor drainage at the level of the trabecular meshwork, preventing its exit into Schlemm's canal.
  • This obstruction generates a sudden increase in intraocular pressure due to the accumulation of aqueous humor in the anterior chamber.
  • The most common mechanism is pupillary block, where the The iris rests against the lens, preventing the passage of aqueous humor from the posterior to the anterior chamber.
  • The differential diagnosis includes neovascular glaucoma, where there are neovessels in the iris and angle, and phacomorphic glaucoma due to intumescent lens.

items.foco: Cause: pupillary block due to iris in lens
items.title: Main cause
items.contentHtml:
Cause: pupillary block due to iris in lens

  • The immediate cause is pupillary block, in which the peripheral portion of the iris bulges forward (iris in lens) and occludes the trabecular meshwork.
  • This phenomenon occurs when the pressure in the posterior chamber exceeds that of the anterior chamber, due to resistance to the flow of aqueous humor through the pupil.
  • Factors predisposing anatomical features: narrow anterior chamber, thick lens (advanced age) and anterior insertion of the iris.
  • Pupillary block is confirmed in gonioscopy by observing a closed angle with iridotrabecular contact and absence of angle structures.

items.title: Key symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Symptoms: sudden intense eye pain

  • The cardinal symptom is sudden intense eye pain, deep and stabbing, which can radiate to the ipsilateral hemicrania.
  • It is accompanied by nausea and vomiting due to the vagal reflex, simulating an acute abdomen or a migraine.
  • Others symptoms: blurred vision, halos around lights (due to corneal edema), and eye redness.
  • The pain is usually so intense that the patient seeks emergency care; The absence of pain does not rule out the condition if there is subacute or chronic closure.

items.title: pupillary sign
items.contentHtml:
Sign: medium and fixed mydriasis

  • The pathognomonic sign is medium and fixed mydriasis (vertical oval pupil), due to ischemia of the pupillary sphincter due to high intraocular pressure.
  • The pupil does not react to light or accommodation, which differentiates it from open angle glaucoma where the pupil is usually normal.
  • In stages early there may be moderate mydriasis; If the pressure is controlled quickly, the pupil can partially recover its reactivity.
  • Fixed mydriasis also appears in other ischemic optic neuropathies, but the context of pain and elevated intraocular pressure points to angle closure glaucoma.

items.title: Corneal finding
items.contentHtml:
Finding: edematous and opaque cornea

  • The edematous and opaque cornea is due to endothelial failure due to high intraocular pressure (>40 mmHg), which causes stromal imbibition of aqueous humor.
  • Corneal edema reduces transparency, giving the appearance of milky or frosted cornea, and makes visualization of the fundus difficult.
  • Epithelial vesicles (bullae) can be observed in severe cases, and folds in Descemet's membrane.
  • The resolution of edema occurs by decreasing intraocular pressure; If it persists, it suggests irreversible endothelial damage.

items.title: intraocular pressure
items.contentHtml:
Intraocular pressure: elevated >40 mmHg

  • Intraocular pressure rises sharply above 40 mmHg, and can reach 60-80 mmHg in the acute attack.
  • The normal value of intraocular pressure is 10-21 mmHg; Figures >30 mmHg already compromise the perfusion of the optic nerve.
  • The measurement is performed with applanation tonometry (Goldmann) or Perkins tonometer; If the cornea is very edematous, a pneumotonometer can be used.
  • An intraocular pressure >40 mmHg maintained for hours causes irreversible ischemic damage to the optic nerve.

items.foco: Risk factor: hyperopia and narrow anterior chamber
items.title: Anatomical risk factors
items.contentHtml:
Risk factor: hyperopia and narrow anterior chamber

  • Hyperopia is a major risk factor because the hyperopic eye has a relatively large lens and a narrower anterior chamber.
  • The narrow anterior chamber (depth <2.5 mm by biometry) predisposes to iridocorneal contact and pupillary block.
  • Others factors: age >40 years, female sex (4:1), Asian or Inuit race, family history of angle-closure glaucoma.
  • Evaluation of anterior chamber depth is performed with the slit lamp (Van Herick method) and confirmed with gonioscopy.

items.title: Definitive diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis: gonioscopy confirms angle closure

  • Gonioscopy is the gold standard to confirm angle closure, directly visualizing the trabecular meshwork and the iris.
  • It is classified according to the Shaffer system: grade 0 (complete closure) to grade 4 (open angle); in acute angle-closure glaucoma, grade 0-1 is observed.
  • Gonioscopic signs: iridotrabecular contact, absence of scleral spur and ciliary processes.
  • Gonioscopy should be performed in the contralateral eye to evaluate the risk of closure; If the angle is narrow, prophylactic iridotomy is indicated.

items.title: Acute medical treatment
items.contentHtml:
Acute treatment: intravenous acetazolamide

  • The first pharmacological line is intravenous acetazolamide at a dose of 500 mg, a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reduces the production of aqueous humor.
  • Intravenous mannitol 20% (1-2 g/kg) is also administered as an osmotic agent to rapidly reduce pressure intraocular.
  • Topical route: timolol 0.5% every 12 hours (beta blocker) and apraclonidine 0.5% (alpha-2 agonist) to reduce the production of aqueous humor.
  • Topical pilocarpine 2% is used only if the intraocular pressure has fallen to <40 mmHg, since with Pupillary sphincter ischemia is not effective and may worsen the blockage.

items.foco: Maneuver: topical pilocarpine in closed angle
items.title: Pilocarpine maneuver
items.contentHtml:
Maneuver: topical pilocarpine at angle closure

  • Topical pilocarpine 2% is instilled into the affected eye only after the intraocular pressure has decreased to <40 mmHg with systemic medical treatment.
  • Its mechanism is to contract the pupillary sphincter (miosis), tightening the peripheral iris and opening the angle camerular.
  • If administered with very high intraocular pressure (>40 mmHg), sphincter ischemia prevents miosis and may exacerbate pupillary block.
  • Dose: 1 drop every 15 minutes up to 3 doses; do not use more than 3 doses due to risk of systemic toxicity (bradycardia, bronchospasm).

items.title: Irreversible complication
items.contentHtml:
Complication: irreversible optic nerve atrophy

  • The most serious complication is irreversible optic nerve atrophy, secondary to ischemia due to vascular compression and direct axonal damage due to elevated intraocular pressure.
  • The damage manifests as visual field loss (arcuate scotomas, nasal defect) and optic atrophy on fundoscopy (disc pallor). optic).
  • If intraocular pressure is not controlled within hours, visual loss can be permanent and complete (amaurosis).
  • Other complications: peripheral anterior synechiae, cataracts, chronic angle-closure glaucoma and corneal decompensation.

items.foco: Prevention: laser iridotomy in the contralateral eye
items.title: Prevention in contralateral eye
items.contentHtml:
Prevention: laser iridotomy in the contralateral eye

  • In patients with unilateral acute angle-closure glaucoma, prophylactic laser iridotomy should be performed in the contralateral eye to prevent an acute attack.
  • Nd:YAG laser iridotomy creates a hole in the peripheral iris, equalizing interchamber pressures and eliminating pupillary block.
  • Indication: narrow angle (degree 1-2 of Shaffer) or intermittent closure documented by gonioscopy.
  • The procedure is performed on an outpatient basis; The most common complication is iris bleeding, which is usually self-limiting.
